## Brief: B-001 — Establish workspace, crate structure, and error types

Set up the Cargo workspace and crate scaffolding so that every subsequent brief has a compilable foundation to build on. Establish the error type hierarchy so that all modules return values rather than panics from day one. This is the foundation everything else depends on — until it lands, no other brief can compile.

## Requirements

### R1: Workspace Cargo.toml configuration

The workspace Cargo.toml at the repo root SHALL declare resolver = "2" and members = ["crates/beamr", "crates/beamr-cli"]. It SHALL NOT list any other crates as members (beamr-meridian is a future brief).

Modify: Cargo.toml

Acceptance:
- Cargo.toml [workspace] members array contains exactly "crates/beamr" and "crates/beamr-cli"
- resolver = "2" is set

Checklist:
- C1: Workspace Cargo.toml at repo root declares members: beamr, beamr-cli

### R2: beamr crate configuration

The beamr crate at crates/beamr/ SHALL use edition 2024 and SHALL NOT declare dependencies on any Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates. It MAY declare dependencies on third-party utility crates (e.g. dashmap, crossbeam) as needed by later briefs.

Modify: crates/beamr/Cargo.toml

Acceptance:
- crates/beamr/Cargo.toml has edition = "2024"
- crates/beamr/Cargo.toml [dependencies] section contains no entries matching meridian*, yggdrasil*, norn*, or libyggd*

Checklist:
- C2: beamr crate exists at crates/beamr/ with edition 2024
- C5: beamr has no dependencies on any Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates

Stories:
- S14: As the Meridian runtime, I want beamr to have zero dependencies on Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates so that the VM is testable and buildable in isolation.

### R3: beamr-cli crate configuration

The beamr-cli crate at crates/beamr-cli/ SHALL be a binary crate with edition 2024 and SHALL depend on the beamr crate only. It SHALL NOT depend on beamr-meridian or any Meridian crates.

Modify: crates/beamr-cli/Cargo.toml

Acceptance:
- crates/beamr-cli/Cargo.toml has edition = "2024"
- crates/beamr-cli/Cargo.toml [dependencies] lists only beamr = { path = "../beamr" }
- [[bin]] section defines name = "beamr"

Checklist:
- C3: beamr-cli crate exists at crates/beamr-cli/ as a binary crate with edition 2024
- C6: beamr-cli depends on beamr only

### R4: Public module declarations in lib.rs

crates/beamr/src/lib.rs SHALL declare public modules: atom, loader, term, process, interpreter, scheduler, gc, mailbox, supervision, native, hook, timer, module, error. It SHALL NOT contain any function, trait, struct, or enum definitions — only pub mod and pub use declarations.

Modify: crates/beamr/src/lib.rs

Acceptance:
- lib.rs contains exactly 14 pub mod declarations matching the list above
- lib.rs contains no fn, struct, enum, trait, or impl blocks
- Every declared module directory/file exists (even if scaffold-only)

Checklist:
- C4: beamr/src/lib.rs declares public modules: atom, loader, term, process, interpreter, scheduler, gc, mailbox, supervision, native, hook, timer, module, error

### R5: LoadError type

WHEN the loader encounters a failure (invalid magic bytes, missing chunk, malformed instruction, unresolved import) THE SYSTEM SHALL return a LoadError variant from beamr::error. LoadError SHALL implement std::fmt::Display with a human-readable description and std::error::Error. It SHALL NOT panic on any loader failure.

Modify: crates/beamr/src/error.rs

Acceptance:
- LoadError is a public enum in beamr::error
- Variants include at minimum: InvalidFormat, MissingChunk(String), DecodeError(String), ValidationError(String)
- LoadError implements Display and std::error::Error
- format!("{}", LoadError::MissingChunk("Atom".into())) produces a non-empty human-readable string

Checklist:
- C7: LoadError enum defined in beamr::error with variants for loader failures

Stories:
- S22: As an implementation agent, I want explicit error types rather than panics for all loader and interpreter failures so that test failures produce actionable diagnostics.

### R6: ExecError type

WHEN the interpreter or a process encounters a runtime failure (badmatch, function_clause, undef, badarith) THE SYSTEM SHALL return an ExecError variant from beamr::error. ExecError SHALL define the variant shape (enum variants with names) but SHALL use opaque placeholder fields — concrete payload types (e.g. Atom, Term) are defined by later briefs (B-005 terms). ExecError SHALL implement Display and std::error::Error. It SHALL NOT lock variant payloads to String types that would require breaking changes when terms land.

Modify: crates/beamr/src/error.rs

Acceptance:
- ExecError is a public enum in beamr::error
- Variants exist for at minimum: Badmatch, FunctionClause, Undef, Badarith, UserExit
- Variant payloads use placeholder types (unit or a simple wrapper) — no String fields for module/function/reason that will later become Atom/Term
- ExecError implements Display and std::error::Error
- format!("{}", ExecError::Badarith) produces a non-empty human-readable string

Checklist:
- C8: ExecError enum defined in beamr::error with variants for runtime failures

Stories:
- S22: As an implementation agent, I want explicit error types rather than panics for all loader and interpreter failures so that test failures produce actionable diagnostics.

### R7: Top-level BeamrError unifying type

beamr::error SHALL define a BeamrError enum that wraps LoadError and ExecError. It SHALL implement From<LoadError> and From<ExecError> for ergonomic ? usage. It SHALL implement Display and std::error::Error.

Modify: crates/beamr/src/error.rs

Acceptance:
- BeamrError is a public enum with variants Load(LoadError) and Exec(ExecError)
- BeamrError::from(LoadError::InvalidFormat) compiles and produces BeamrError::Load(_)
- BeamrError::from(ExecError::Badarith) compiles and produces BeamrError::Exec(_)
- BeamrError implements Display and std::error::Error

Checklist:
- C9: BeamrError enum defined in beamr::error wrapping LoadError and ExecError with From impls

Stories:
- S22: As an implementation agent, I want explicit error types rather than panics for all loader and interpreter failures so that test failures produce actionable diagnostics.

### R8: Workspace compiles and passes clippy

WHEN all crate configurations and error types are in place THE SYSTEM SHALL pass cargo check --workspace and cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings with zero errors and zero warnings.


Acceptance:
- cargo check --workspace exits with code 0
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings exits with code 0

Checklist:
- C10: cargo check --workspace passes clean
- C11: cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes clean

## Boundaries

- SHALL NOT add any third-party dependencies to beamr/Cargo.toml (dependencies like dashmap are added by the briefs that need them)
- SHALL NOT implement any logic in lib.rs beyond module declarations
- SHALL NOT add beamr-meridian to the workspace members (future brief scope)
- SHALL NOT implement loader, interpreter, or process logic — only the error type definitions they will use

Full design document: docs/design/beamr/DESIGN.md

1	---
2	type: design
3	cluster: beamr
4	title: Beamr — BEAM Process Runtime in Rust
5	---
6	
7	# Beamr — BEAM Process Runtime in Rust
8	
9	## Intention
10	
11	Beamr is the floor Meridian's workflows and agents stand on. When it's
12	done, a Gleam workflow definition compiles to `.beam` bytecode, loads
13	into a Rust runtime, and executes with the properties that make the BEAM
14	the best concurrent runtime ever built: preemptive fairness (no task
15	starves others), per-process fault isolation (one crash doesn't sink the
16	ship), supervision trees (the system heals itself), and hot code loading
17	(deploy without restart).
18	
19	It should feel inevitable. A workflow author writes typed Gleam, the
20	compiler catches errors, the runtime handles concurrency and recovery
21	structurally. The author never thinks about threads, locks, retry logic,
22	or process lifecycle — those are the runtime's problem. Native Rust
23	operations (git, AST-merge, dependency graph, diagnostics) are plain
24	function calls inside the VM, not foreign invocations across a boundary.
25	
26	The reduction-boundary hook — the thing that makes this more than an
27	academic exercise — gives Meridian a guaranteed, regular checkpoint on
28	every running process, regardless of what that process is doing. A live
29	conscience, not a post-mortem.
30	
31	## Problem
32	
33	Workflow definitions today are either configuration (YAML — write-once,
34	debug-never, no types, no composition, no tests) or dynamically typed
35	scripts (Rhai — runtime errors 45 minutes into a run, no LSP, no
36	tooling). Neither gives you compile-time safety, composable pipelines,
37	or structural fault recovery.
38	
39	Rust's async ecosystem cannot provide the concurrency model workflows
40	need. Tokio tasks are cooperatively scheduled — a task that doesn't
41	`.await` pins a core indefinitely. There is no preemptive fairness, no
42	per-task fault isolation, no supervision. A panicking task can poison
43	shared state. A CPU-bound task starves its neighbours.
44	
45	The BEAM has all of these properties, but it's written in C, has no
46	clean embedding story for Rust, and carries 30 years of Erlang/OTP
47	baggage. Existing attempts to rebuild it (Lumen/Firefly — abandoned,
48	targeted LLVM native compilation; AtomVM — C, targets embedded/IoT;
49	Lunatic — WASM runtime, not a BEAM interpreter) either aimed too wide
50	or solved a different problem.
51	
52	The gap: a Rust runtime with the BEAM's execution model, scoped to what
53	Gleam workflows actually need, where native Rust operations are
54	first-class citizens inside the VM rather than foreign functions fighting
55	across a C boundary.
56	
57	## Solution
58	
59	### Design Principles
60	
61	1. **Beamr depends on nothing of Meridian's. Meridian depends on beamr.**
62	   The VM is a self-contained machine that knows how to load bytecode,
63	   run processes, and call functions you hand it. It does not know what
64	   git is, what Yggdrasil is, or what a diagnostic is. Those plug in
65	   from outside.
66	
67	2. **Demand-driven scope.** The loader's unresolved-import report is the
68	   work queue. Every external function a `.beam` file calls is listed in
69	   its import table. When the loader can't resolve one, that's a signal:
70	   "implement this BIF." The scope of BIF implementation physically
71	   cannot grow beyond what the workflows demand. The import table is the
72	   leash.
73	
74	3. **No silent failures.** A process that encounters an error crashes
75	   visibly. Supervisors handle recovery structurally. No swallowed
76	   errors, no default fallbacks, no "log and continue."
77	
78	4. **Per-process isolation is load-bearing.** Every process owns its
79	   heap, its stack, its mailbox. Nothing is shared. One process crashing
80	   affects only itself and its linked processes. This property is not
81	   aspirational — it is the architectural invariant the rest of the
82	   system depends on.
83	
84	5. **Fairness is guaranteed, not hoped for.** The interpreter counts
85	   reductions. When the budget hits zero, the process yields. No process
86	   can starve others, even in a tight computation loop with no I/O.
87	
88	### Module Layout
89	
90	The runtime is organised as modules inside a single `beamr` crate.
91	These are tightly coupled — the interpreter touches terms, heap,
92	scheduler, and mailbox constantly — and splitting them into separate
93	crates would add ceremony without value at this stage.
94	
95	**Atom table** — global interned string table. Lock-free concurrent map.
96	Every name in the system (module names, function names, atoms like `ok`
97	and `error`) lives here exactly once. Comparing two atoms is comparing
98	two integers. The loader populates it; the interpreter consults it;
99	terms can be atoms.
100	
101	**Loader** — reads `.beam` files (the chunked binary format the Gleam
102	toolchain produces via `erlc`). Parses atom tables, instruction streams,
103	import/export tables, literal tables, string tables, lambda tables.
104	Resolves imports against loaded modules and the BIF registry. Produces
105	runnable `Module` values. Unresolved imports become the demand-driven
106	work queue.
107	
108	**Terms** — tagged 64-bit values. Immediates (small integers, atoms,
109	pids, nil) fit in a single word. Boxed values (tuples, lists, binaries,
110	big integers, floats, closures, maps, references) are pointers into the
111	process-local heap. Low-bit tagging, not NaN-boxing — the hot path is
112	integers, atoms, and pointers, not floats.
113	
114	**Processes** — the unit of execution and isolation. Each owns a heap,
115	stack, mailbox, reduction counter, link/monitor sets, trap-exit flag,
116	and status. Spawning is microsecond-scale — no OS thread, no allocation
117	beyond the initial small heap. A process cannot reach into another's
118	memory.
119	
120	**Interpreter** — the execution loop. Fetch, decode, execute, decrement
121	reduction counter. When the counter hits zero, save state and yield.
122	Implements the subset of BEAM opcodes that Gleam actually emits — not
123	all ~170. The binary-matching instruction family is the hardest subset
124	and gets focused attention.
125	
126	**Scheduler** — N OS threads, each with a run queue of ready processes.
127	Pick the highest-priority process, execute it for a reduction budget,
128	handle the result (yielded → back of queue; waiting → wait set; exited →
129	cleanup). Work stealing: idle schedulers take half the processes from
130	the busiest queue. No async runtime in the scheduler hot path — plain
131	OS threads plus lock-free work-stealing queues.
132	
133	**Memory and GC** — per-process generational copying collector. Young
134	generation (nursery) collected frequently; old generation collected
135	rarely. Heap sizes start small (~2KB) so collection is microseconds.
136	GC affects only the process being collected — no stop-the-world, ever.
137	Messages are copied into the receiver's heap on delivery, preserving
138	isolation.
139	
140	**Mailbox** — per-process message queue. Lock-free MPSC. Selective
141	receive: the process pattern-matches against the mailbox, taking the
142	first message that matches and deferring the rest. A save pointer tracks
143	scan progress to avoid rescanning. When no message matches, the process
144	suspends until mail arrives or a timeout fires.
145	
146	**Supervision primitives** — links (bidirectional, fatal by default),
147	monitors (unidirectional, non-fatal), exit signals (carry the exit
148	reason along links and monitors), and the trap-exit flag (converts fatal
149	exit signals into messages). The VM provides these four primitives;
150	actual supervisor behaviour is Gleam library code (gleam_otp) running on
151	the VM like any other program.
152	
153	**Native function interface** — a registry where Rust functions are
154	registered under module:function/arity names. When the interpreter hits
155	a call to a registered native, it invokes the Rust function directly —
156	same process, no IPC, no serialisation. BIFs (built-in, ship with the
157	VM) and NIFs (registered by the host) use the same mechanism but have
158	different ownership: BIFs are beamr's responsibility, NIFs are
159	Meridian's.
160	
161	**Dirty schedulers** — a separate thread pool for native calls that
162	genuinely take time (git push, cargo build). Long-running work goes
163	here so normal scheduler threads stay free and fair. Same concept as
164	the BEAM's dirty schedulers, for the same reason.
165	
166	**Timer wheel** — handles `receive after` timeouts, periodic ticks, and
167	step deadline enforcement. Wakes suspended processes when their timeout
168	fires.
169	
170	**Reduction-boundary hook** — the seam where Meridian listens. Every
171	time a process yields (budget exhausted or blocking on receive), the
172	hook fires. Meridian's conventions-and-diagnostics pipeline can inspect
173	what just happened and intervene before the process resumes. This is the
174	same primitive that norn uses at the tool boundary and norn-memory uses
175	for resonance, but at the lowest altitude — the interpreter loop itself.
176	A process cannot outrun the tap on the shoulder.
177	
178	### Integration Points
179	
180	**Gleam toolchain** — Gleam compiles to Erlang source; `erlc` compiles
181	that to `.beam` bytecode. Beamr loads the `.beam` output. The bytecode
182	format is stable on Erlang's release cadence.
183	
184	**Meridian (via beamr-meridian, later)** — Yggdrasil operations (git,
185	merge, graph, tracking, worktree), Meridian operations (messaging,
186	storage, workflow dispatch), and filesystem operations are registered as
187	NIFs. The reduction-boundary hook is wired to norn's conventions engine.
188	
189	**Gleam OTP library** — supervision trees, actors, typed processes. Runs
190	as regular Gleam code on the VM. The VM provides the primitives (links,
191	monitors, exit signals); gleam_otp builds the patterns.
192	
193	### Numbered Decisions
194	
195	**D1 — Loader is a module inside the core crate, not a separate crate.**
196	The loader's outputs (Module, Term, Atom) are runtime types. Splitting
197	it out creates a dependency problem: either the loader depends on core
198	(negating the independence) or a shared-types crate is needed (which
199	becomes a gravity well). Start with the loader as a module; extract
200	later only if the seam proves itself. Don't solve a dependency problem
201	you haven't created yet.
202	
203	**D2 — Atom table lives in the core, loader accepts a handle.**
204	The atom table is a runtime concern that the loader happens to populate.
205	Keeping it in core avoids a micro-crate and keeps ownership clear.
206	
207	**D3 — No async runtime in the scheduler.** The scheduler uses plain OS
208	threads and lock-free work-stealing queues. Tokio enters only at the
209	edges (file I/O, network I/O) via dirty schedulers. The BEAM's green
210	threads are our interpreter loop, not async tasks. Keeping tokio out of
211	the hot path keeps the fairness story simple and ours.
212	
213	**D4 — Low-bit term tagging, not NaN-boxing.** The hot path is integers,
214	atoms, and pointers. NaN-boxing optimises for float-heavy workloads,
215	which this is not. Classic low-bit tagging matches the BEAM's own scheme
216	and the assumptions baked into the compiled bytecode.
217	
218	**D5 — Implement only the opcodes Gleam emits.** The BEAM has ~170
219	opcodes. Gleam uses a subset. The loader's import and instruction
220	analysis tells us exactly which opcodes appear in real `.beam` files
221	from `gleam build`. Implement those; leave the rest as explicit
222	unimplemented errors that name the missing opcode.
223	
224	**D6 — BIFs are demand-driven.** The set of built-in functions to
225	implement is determined by the loader's unresolved-import report, not
226	by surveying the BEAM's BIF table. If no workflow imports it, it
227	doesn't exist yet. The import table is the leash.
228	
229	**D7 — Supervision is library code, not VM machinery.** The VM provides
230	four primitives: links, monitors, exit signals, and trap-exit.
231	Supervisor strategies (one_for_one, one_for_all, rest_for_one, restart
232	limits) are implemented in Gleam via gleam_otp, running on the VM like
233	any other code. No special VM support for supervisors.
234	
235	**D8 — Messages are copied between processes.** Send copies the term
236	into the receiver's heap. This preserves per-process isolation
237	absolutely. Large binaries may use reference-counted shared storage
238	as an optimisation, but the semantic model is always copy.
239	
240	**D9 — The reduction-boundary hook is a registration point, not a
241	hard-coded call.** The core provides the seam; what runs in it is
242	registered from outside (by beamr-meridian). The core does not know
243	what diagnostics are. This preserves principle 1.
244	
245	**D10 — Dirty schedulers are a separate thread pool.** Native functions
246	that may block (I/O, git operations, compilation) run on dirty scheduler
247	threads, not normal scheduler threads. The normal scheduler never blocks
248	on native work. The dirty pool size is configurable independently of the
249	normal scheduler thread count.
250	
251	**D11 — Mailbox uses lock-free MPSC.** The per-process mailbox is backed
252	by a lock-free multi-producer single-consumer queue (crossbeam SegQueue
253	or equivalent). Multiple processes can send to the same mailbox
254	concurrently without mutex contention. This is the hot path for message
255	passing — every send touches it — and lock-free is the only choice that
256	doesn't create a serialisation bottleneck under concurrent sends.
257	
258	## Goals
259	
260	1. Load and execute a `.beam` file produced by `gleam build` — pure

output.content:
258	## Goals
259	
260	1. Load and execute a `.beam` file produced by `gleam build` — pure
261	   Gleam modules (arithmetic, pattern matching, list operations)
262	   execute correctly.
263	
264	2. Spawn processes and pass messages between them — mailbox delivery,
265	   selective receive, and process exit all function correctly.
266	
267	3. Preemptive scheduling across multiple OS threads — a tight
268	   computation loop yields after N reductions; idle schedulers steal
269	   work; every ready process runs at least once per scheduling epoch
270	   (one full pass through all scheduler run queues).
271	
272	4. Per-process garbage collection — minor and major collections
273	   triggered by allocation pressure; GC does not affect other processes;
274	   all term references survive collection intact.
275	
276	5. Register a Rust function and call it from Gleam — arguments decode
277	   correctly, return values encode correctly, errors propagate as
278	   process exit reasons.
279	
280	6. Fault isolation via supervision primitives — process linking
281	   propagates exit signals, monitors deliver DOWN messages, trap-exit
282	   converts signals to messages. These primitives are sufficient to
283	   enable OTP-style supervision patterns (one_for_one restart, restart
284	   limits, supervisor escalation) when driven by gleam_otp library code.
285	
286	7. The reduction-boundary hook fires on every process yield and can
287	   be used by an external registrant to inspect and intervene.
288	
289	## Non-Goals
290	
291	- **Hot code loading.** Valuable but not needed for the initial runtime.
292	  The module registry supports a single version per module. Hot loading
293	  (two-version registry, fully-qualified-call version switching, old
294	  code purging) is future work.
295	
296	- **Distribution protocol.** Beamr is single-node. No Erlang distribution,
297	  no inter-node communication, no epmd. Cross-instance communication
298	  goes through Meridian's exchange, not BEAM distribution.
299	
300	- **ETS (Erlang Term Storage).** Concurrent term tables are useful but
301	  not required for workflow execution. If needed, Rust's concurrent
302	  maps (dashmap) provide the same capability at the native layer.
303	
304	- **Full OTP stdlib.** Beamr implements the BIFs that Gleam's stdlib
305	  and gleam_otp actually import, not the full `erlang` module surface.
306	  Demand-driven per D6.
307	
308	- **JIT compilation.** The interpreter is sufficient for workflow
309	  scripts. A JIT (like BEAM's OTP 24+ JIT) would optimise compute-
310	  heavy paths but adds enormous complexity for marginal workflow
311	  benefit.
312	
313	- **Port drivers.** BEAM's port system for external process I/O. Use
314	  NIFs and dirty schedulers instead.
315	
316	- **Process dictionary.** The BEAM provides a per-process key-value
317	  store (`put`/`get`). Gleam discourages its use and gleam_otp does
318	  not depend on it. If specific `gleam_erlang` functions require it,
319	  add a simple `HashMap<Term, Term>` to the process struct at that
320	  point — but it is not in initial scope.
321	
322	- **Tracing and debugging BIFs.** Add incrementally if needed. Not
323	  in initial scope.
324	
325	## Structure
326	
327	```
328	crates/
329	├── beamr/                         — the VM
330	│   ├── Cargo.toml
331	│   └── src/
332	│       ├── lib.rs                 — public API: Vm::new(), load_module(), spawn()
333	│       ├── error.rs               — crate error types
334	│       ├── atom/
335	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — AtomTable, Atom handle type
336	│       │   └── table.rs           — lock-free concurrent intern map
337	│       ├── loader/
338	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — load_beam() entry point
339	│       │   ├── parser.rs          — chunked .beam format parser
340	│       │   ├── decode.rs          — bytecode instruction decoder
341	│       │   └── validate.rs        — instruction operand validation
342	│       ├── term/
343	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — Term type, tag constants, immediate ops
344	│       │   ├── boxed.rs           — boxed term headers and accessors
345	│       │   ├── binary.rs          — binary/bitstring representation
346	│       │   └── compare.rs         — term ordering and equality
347	│       ├── process/
348	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — Process struct, lifecycle
349	│       │   ├── heap.rs            — per-process heap allocator
350	│       │   ├── stack.rs           — call stack frames
351	│       │   └── registry.rs        — process name registry
352	│       ├── interpreter/
353	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — execute() loop, reduction counting
354	│       │   ├── opcodes.rs         — opcode dispatch table
355	│       │   └── pattern.rs         — pattern match instruction support
356	│       ├── scheduler/
357	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — scheduler thread pool, main loop
358	│       │   ├── run_queue.rs       — priority run queues
359	│       │   ├── steal.rs           — work-stealing logic
360	│       │   └── dirty.rs           — dirty scheduler thread pool
361	│       ├── gc/
362	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — GC entry point, trigger logic
363	│       │   ├── minor.rs           — nursery → old generation copy
364	│       │   └── major.rs           — full heap compaction
365	│       ├── mailbox/
366	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — Mailbox struct, send/receive
367	│       │   └── selective.rs       — selective receive + save pointer
368	│       ├── supervision/
369	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — link/monitor/exit-signal dispatch
370	│       │   ├── link.rs            — bidirectional link management
371	│       │   └── monitor.rs         — unidirectional monitor management
372	│       ├── native/
373	│       │   ├── mod.rs             — NIF/BIF registry
374	│       │   └── bifs.rs            — built-in function implementations
375	│       ├── hook.rs                — reduction-boundary hook registration
376	│       ├── module.rs              — module registry (single-version)
377	│       └── timer.rs               — timer wheel for timeouts
378	├── beamr-cli/                     — thin runner for bring-up
379	│   ├── Cargo.toml
380	│   └── src/
381	│       └── main.rs               — load .beam, register natives, run
382	└── beamr-meridian/                — (later) Meridian integration
383	    ├── Cargo.toml
384	    └── src/
385	        ├── lib.rs                 — registration entry point
386	        ├── yggdrasil.rs           — git, merge, graph, tracking NIFs
387	        ├── meridian.rs            — messaging, storage, workflow NIFs
388	        ├── fs.rs                  — filesystem NIFs
389	        └── hook.rs                — wire reduction hook to conventions
390	```
391	
392	## Constraints
393	
394	- **No file over 500 lines.** If a module approaches this, split by
395	  responsibility. The interpreter's opcode dispatch is the most likely
396	  candidate — split by opcode family if needed.
397	
398	- **No `.unwrap()` or `.expect()` outside `#[cfg(test)]` code.** All
399	  production paths use `?` or explicit error handling. A process
400	  encountering an error exits with a reason — it does not panic the
401	  scheduler thread. This rule is mechanically enforceable via clippy
402	  lint. The only acceptable panics are in test code and in truly
403	  unrecoverable states (out of memory, which is `alloc::handle_alloc_error`).
404	
405	- **No flat module structures.** Related code lives in directories with
406	  `mod.rs` files, not as a pile of files in `src/`. Single-file modules
407	  at the `src/` root are acceptable when they have no sub-responsibilities
408	  (e.g. `hook.rs`, `module.rs`, `timer.rs`). The structure section above
409	  is the canonical layout.
410	
411	- **No unsafe without justification.** Unsafe blocks are acceptable for
412	  term tagging, heap pointer manipulation, and lock-free data
413	  structures — the core of a VM implementation. Each unsafe block must
414	  have a safety comment explaining the invariant it relies on.
415	
416	- **Per-process isolation is non-negotiable.** No shared mutable state
417	  between processes. No process can read or write another's heap. If a
418	  design decision would weaken isolation, the decision is wrong.
419	
420	- **The hook is passive.** The reduction-boundary hook is a registration
421	  point. The core fires it and waits for a response. It does not make
422	  decisions about what to do — the registrant does. The core stays
423	  ignorant of Meridian's concerns.

192	- [ ] **C155** — A native call marked as dirty causes the process to be migrated to the dirty scheduler pool for the duration of the call
193	- [ ] **C156** — The set of required native functions is derived from the loader's unresolved-import report, not hardcoded
194	- [ ] **C157** — Minimum BIFs for Gate 1: erlang:+/2, erlang:-/2, erlang:*/2, erlang:div/2, erlang:rem/2, erlang:</2, erlang:>=/2, erlang:=:=/2, erlang:=/=/2, erlang:error/1, erlang:display/1
195	
196	## Reduction Boundary Hook
197	
198	- [ ] **C158** — A configurable hook callback fires at process yield points (reduction budget exhausted or blocking on receive)
199	- [ ] **C159** — The hook receives the process pid, its current module/function/arity, and the reduction count consumed
200	- [ ] **C160** — The hook can return 'continue' (process resumes normally) or 'suspend' (process is held until explicitly resumed)
201	- [ ] **C161** — The hook callback is set per VM instance and can be changed at runtime
202	- [ ] **C162** — When no hook is registered, the hook registration slot is None and the yield path skips hook invocation
203	- [ ] **C163** — The hook cannot modify the process's registers, heap, or mailbox — it is read-only inspection plus a continue/suspend decision
204	
205	## Timer
206	
207	- [ ] **C164** — Timer wheel supports scheduling, cancellation, and expiry of timeouts with O(1) insertion and cancellation
208	- [ ] **C165** — wait_timeout instruction registers a timer; if it expires before a matching message arrives, the process resumes at the timeout label
209	- [ ] **C166** — Timer is cancelled automatically when the process receives a matching message before expiry
210	- [ ] **C167** — erlang:send_after/3 BIF sends a message to a process after a delay
211	- [ ] **C168** — erlang:start_timer/3 BIF sends a {timeout, Ref, Msg} tuple after a delay
212	- [ ] **C169** — erlang:cancel_timer/1 BIF cancels a pending timer and returns remaining time
213	- [ ] **C170** — Timer resolution is millisecond-granularity or better
214	
215	## CLI
216	
217	- [ ] **C171** — beamr-cli binary accepts a path to a .beam file and a module:function/arity to execute
218	- [ ] **C172** — Loads the specified .beam file via beamr::loader and spawns a process calling the specified function
219	- [ ] **C173** — Registers basic I/O natives: erlang:display/1 prints a term to stdout
220	- [ ] **C174** — Prints the exit reason when the initial process terminates

arguments.tool_use_description: Read user stories referenced by brief, especially S14 and S22, for implementation intent.

output.content:
1	# Beamr — User Stories
2	
3	## Workflow Author — Writing Gleam Workflows
4	
5	**S1.** As a workflow author, I want to write workflow steps as typed Gleam functions that compile to .beam bytecode so that I get compile-time type checking on my entire pipeline.
6	
7	**S2.** As a workflow author, I want each workflow step to run in its own isolated process so that a crash in one step does not corrupt the state of another.
8	
9	**S3.** As a workflow author, I want to use Gleam's pattern matching and case expressions for routing so that the compiler catches unhandled cases at build time, not at runtime.
10	
11	**S4.** As a workflow author, I want to call Rust-native operations (git, AST merge, dependency graph) from Gleam via registered functions so that I get zero-overhead access to the host's capabilities.
12	
13	**S5.** As a workflow author, I want workflow steps to communicate via typed message passing so that data flows between steps without shared mutable state or serialization.
14	
15	**S6.** As a workflow author, I want to define supervision trees over my workflow steps so that failed steps are automatically restarted according to a declared strategy.
16	
17	**S7.** As a workflow author, I want to set timeouts on receive expressions so that a step waiting for input does not hang indefinitely if its dependency fails silently.
18	
19	## Meridian Runtime — Embedding beamr for Workflow Execution
20	
21	**S8.** As the Meridian runtime, I want to create a beamr VM instance, load .beam modules, and spawn processes from Rust so that I can embed workflow execution in the server process.
22	
23	**S9.** As the Meridian runtime, I want to register Rust functions as callable BIFs and NIFs before loading modules so that Gleam code can call Yggdrasil, git, and Meridian operations.
24	
25	**S10.** As the Meridian runtime, I want a reduction-boundary hook that fires at every process yield so that the conventions-and-diagnostics pipeline can inspect and intervene in running workflows.
26	
27	**S11.** As the Meridian runtime, I want long-running native calls to execute on a dirty scheduler pool so that they do not block the normal scheduler threads or starve other processes.
28	
29	**S12.** As the Meridian runtime, I want the scheduler to use one thread per CPU core with work-stealing so that all cores are utilized and no core sits idle while others are backlogged.
30	
31	**S13.** As the Meridian runtime, I want the VM to guarantee preemptive fairness via reduction counting so that no single workflow can monopolize CPU and starve other workflows.
32	
33	**S14.** As the Meridian runtime, I want beamr to have zero dependencies on Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates so that the VM is testable and buildable in isolation.
34	
35	**S15.** As the Meridian runtime, I want to shut down the VM cleanly, terminating all scheduler threads and processes, so that there are no leaked threads or orphan resources.
36	
37	## Implementation Agent — Building beamr from Briefs
38	
39	**S16.** As an implementation agent, I want the loader to be a well-encapsulated module within the beamr crate so that I can test .beam parsing via module-level tests without needing external harnesses.
40	
41	**S17.** As an implementation agent, I want the loader's unresolved-import report to tell me exactly which BIFs a given .beam file needs so that I build only the natives required, not the entire erlang: module.
42	
43	**S18.** As an implementation agent, I want each validation gate to have a concrete test suite so that I can verify my work against defined pass/fail criteria before moving to the next gate.
44	
45	**S19.** As an implementation agent, I want the term representation, heap, and GC to be defined in a single crate with clear module boundaries so that I can understand the term lifecycle without cross-crate indirection.
46	
47	**S20.** As an implementation agent, I want the interpreter's opcode dispatch to be organized by instruction category so that I can implement opcodes incrementally (calls first, then branching, then binary matching) without touching unrelated code.
48	
49	**S21.** As an implementation agent, I want the native function interface to accept standard Rust closures so that registering a new BIF requires only one function call with a module name, function name, arity, and closure.
50	
51	**S22.** As an implementation agent, I want explicit error types rather than panics for all loader and interpreter failures so that test failures produce actionable diagnostics.
52	
53	## Reviewer — Verifying beamr Implementation
54	
55	**S23.** As a reviewer, I want each validation gate's test suite to run as a standard cargo test target so that I can verify gate passage with a single command.
56	
57	**S24.** As a reviewer, I want property tests on the GC that verify all reachable terms survive collection unchanged so that I can have confidence in the collector's correctness beyond handwritten cases.
58	
59	**S25.** As a reviewer, I want a fairness test that spawns N processes in a tight computational loop and measures that all N make progress within 2x the expected time so that I can verify no process is starved.
60	
61	**S26.** As a reviewer, I want the loader to produce snapshot-testable output from known .beam files so that I can diff the decoded instructions against a verified baseline.
62	
63	**S27.** As a reviewer, I want to verify that beamr's Cargo.toml has no dependency on any Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crate so that the isolation rule is machine-checkable.
64	
65	**S28.** As a reviewer, I want to run real Gleam test suites (compiled to .beam) on beamr so that correctness is validated against the language's own expectations, not just handwritten bytecode.
66	
67	**S29.** As a reviewer, I want the exit signal propagation to be tested with multi-level link chains so that I can verify cascading failures propagate correctly through supervision trees.
68	
69	**S30.** As a reviewer, I want the reduction boundary hook to be tested with a callback that records every yield so that I can verify the hook fires at every yield point and receives correct process metadata.

8	# B-001: Establish workspace, crate structure, and error types
9	
10	> **Cluster:** beamr
11	> **Checklist:**
12	> - C1 — Workspace Cargo.toml at repo root declares members: beamr, beamr-cli
13	> - C2 — beamr crate exists at crates/beamr/ with edition 2024
14	> - C3 — beamr-cli crate exists at crates/beamr-cli/ as a binary crate with edition 2024
15	> - C4 — beamr/src/lib.rs declares public modules: atom, loader, term, process, interpreter, scheduler, gc, mailbox, supervision, native, hook, timer, module, error
16	> - C5 — beamr has no dependencies on any Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates
17	> - C6 — beamr-cli depends on beamr only
18	> - C7 — LoadError enum defined in beamr::error with variants for loader failures
19	> - C8 — ExecError enum defined in beamr::error with variants for runtime failures
20	> - C9 — BeamrError enum defined in beamr::error wrapping LoadError and ExecError with From impls
21	> - C10 — cargo check --workspace passes clean
22	> - C11 — cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes clean
23	> **Stories:**
24	> - S14 (Meridian Runtime, Embedding beamr for Workflow Execution) — As the Meridian runtime, I want beamr to have zero dependencies on Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates so that the VM is testable and buildable in isolation.
25	> - S22 (Implementation Agent, Building beamr from Briefs) — As an implementation agent, I want explicit error types rather than panics for all loader and interpreter failures so that test failures produce actionable diagnostics.
26	
27	## Purpose
28	
29	Set up the Cargo workspace and crate scaffolding so that every subsequent brief has a compilable foundation to build on. Establish the error type hierarchy so that all modules return values rather than panics from day one. This is the foundation everything else depends on — until it lands, no other brief can compile.
30	
31	## Task
32	
33	The workspace Cargo.toml and both crate Cargo.tomls already exist in the scaffold. Verify and if needed adjust them to match the design: workspace declares beamr and beamr-cli as members, beamr crate uses edition 2024 with no Meridian/Yggdrasil/norn dependencies, beamr-cli depends on beamr only. Verify lib.rs declares all public modules. Define the crate-wide error types in error.rs: LoadError for loader failures, ExecError for interpreter/process failures, and a top-level BeamrError that unifies them. All errors implement std::fmt::Display and std::error::Error. Ensure cargo check and cargo clippy pass clean across the workspace.
34	
35	## Requirements
36	
37	### R1: Workspace Cargo.toml configuration
38	
39	The workspace Cargo.toml at the repo root SHALL declare resolver = "2" and members = ["crates/beamr", "crates/beamr-cli"]. It SHALL NOT list any other crates as members (beamr-meridian is a future brief).
40	
41	**Acceptance:**
42	- Cargo.toml [workspace] members array contains exactly "crates/beamr" and "crates/beamr-cli"
43	- resolver = "2" is set
44	
45	**Files:**
46	- modify: Cargo.toml
47	
48	**Checklist:**
49	- C1 — Workspace Cargo.toml at repo root declares members: beamr, beamr-cli
50	
51	### R2: beamr crate configuration
52	
53	The beamr crate at crates/beamr/ SHALL use edition 2024 and SHALL NOT declare dependencies on any Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates. It MAY declare dependencies on third-party utility crates (e.g. dashmap, crossbeam) as needed by later briefs.
54	
55	**Acceptance:**

57	- crates/beamr/Cargo.toml [dependencies] section contains no entries matching meridian*, yggdrasil*, norn*, or libyggd*
58	
59	**Files:**
60	- modify: crates/beamr/Cargo.toml
61	
62	**Checklist:**
63	- C2 — beamr crate exists at crates/beamr/ with edition 2024
64	- C5 — beamr has no dependencies on any Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates
65	
66	**Stories:**
67	- S14 (Meridian Runtime, Embedding beamr for Workflow Execution) — As the Meridian runtime, I want beamr to have zero dependencies on Meridian, Yggdrasil, or norn crates so that the VM is testable and buildable in isolation.
68	
69	### R3: beamr-cli crate configuration
70	
71	The beamr-cli crate at crates/beamr-cli/ SHALL be a binary crate with edition 2024 and SHALL depend on the beamr crate only. It SHALL NOT depend on beamr-meridian or any Meridian crates.
72	
73	**Acceptance:**

75	- crates/beamr-cli/Cargo.toml [dependencies] lists only beamr = { path = "../beamr" }
76	- [[bin]] section defines name = "beamr"
77

80	
81	**Checklist:**
82	- C3 — beamr-cli crate exists at crates/beamr-cli/ as a binary crate with edition 2024
83	- C6 — beamr-cli depends on beamr only
84	
85	### R4: Public module declarations in lib.rs
86	
87	crates/beamr/src/lib.rs SHALL declare public modules: atom, loader, term, process, interpreter, scheduler, gc, mailbox, supervision, native, hook, timer, module, error. It SHALL NOT contain any function, trait, struct, or enum definitions — only pub mod and pub use declarations.

91	- lib.rs contains no fn, struct, enum, trait, or impl blocks
92	- Every declared module directory/file exists (even if scaffold-only)
93	
94	**Files:**

96	
97	**Checklist:**
98	- C4 — beamr/src/lib.rs declares public modules: atom, loader, term, process, interpreter, scheduler, gc, mailbox, supervision, native, hook, timer, module, error
99	
100	### R5: LoadError type
101	
102	WHEN the loader encounters a failure (invalid magic bytes, missing chunk, malformed instruction, unresolved import) THE SYSTEM SHALL return a LoadError variant from beamr::error. LoadError SHALL implement std::fmt::Display with a human-readable description and std::error::Error. It SHALL NOT panic on any loader failure.
103	
104	**Acceptance:**
105	- LoadError is a public enum in beamr::error
106	- Variants include at minimum: InvalidFormat, MissingChunk(String), DecodeError(String), ValidationError(String)
107	- LoadError implements Display and std::error::Error
108	- format!("{}", LoadError::MissingChunk("Atom".into())) produces a non-empty human-readable string
109

112	
113	**Checklist:**
114	- C7 — LoadError enum defined in beamr::error with variants for loader failures
115	
116	**Stories:**
117	- S22 (Implementation Agent, Building beamr from Briefs) — As an implementation agent, I want explicit error types rather than panics for all loader and interpreter failures so that test failures produce actionable diagnostics.
118	
119	### R6: ExecError type
120	
121	WHEN the interpreter or a process encounters a runtime failure (badmatch, function_clause, undef, badarith) THE SYSTEM SHALL return an ExecError variant from beamr::error. ExecError SHALL define the variant shape (enum variants with names) but SHALL use opaque placeholder fields — concrete payload types (e.g. Atom, Term) are defined by later briefs (B-005 terms). ExecError SHALL implement Display and std::error::Error. It SHALL NOT lock variant payloads to String types that would require breaking changes when terms land.
122	
123	**Acceptance:**
124	- ExecError is a public enum in beamr::error
125	- Variants exist for at minimum: Badmatch, FunctionClause, Undef, Badarith, UserExit
126	- Variant payloads use placeholder types (unit or a simple wrapper) — no String fields for module/function/reason that will later become Atom/Term
127	- ExecError implements Display and std::error::Error
128	- format!("{}", ExecError::Badarith) produces a non-empty human-readable string
129	
130	**Files:**
131	- modify: crates/beamr/src/error.rs
132	
133	**Checklist:**
134	- C8 — ExecError enum defined in beamr::error with variants for runtime failures
135	
136	**Stories:**
137	- S22 (Implementation Agent, Building beamr from Briefs) — As an implementation agent, I want explicit error types rather than panics for all loader and interpreter failures so that test failures produce actionable diagnostics.
138	
139	### R7: Top-level BeamrError unifying type
140	
141	beamr::error SHALL define a BeamrError enum that wraps LoadError and ExecError. It SHALL implement From<LoadError> and From<ExecError> for ergonomic ? usage. It SHALL implement Display and std::error::Error.
142	
143	**Acceptance:**
144	- BeamrError is a public enum with variants Load(LoadError) and Exec(ExecError)
145	- BeamrError::from(LoadError::InvalidFormat) compiles and produces BeamrError::Load(_)
146	- BeamrError::from(ExecError::Badarith) compiles and produces BeamrError::Exec(_)
147	- BeamrError implements Display and std::error::Error
148	
149	**Files:**
150	- modify: crates/beamr/src/error.rs
151	
152	**Checklist:**
153	- C9 — BeamrError enum defined in beamr::error wrapping LoadError and ExecError with From impls
154	
155	**Stories:**
156	- S22 (Implementation Agent, Building beamr from Briefs) — As an implementation agent, I want explicit error types rather than panics for all loader and interpreter failures so that test failures produce actionable diagnostics.
157	
158	### R8: Workspace compiles and passes clippy
159	
160	WHEN all crate configurations and error types are in place THE SYSTEM SHALL pass cargo check --workspace and cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings with zero errors and zero warnings.
161	
162	**Acceptance:**
163	- cargo check --workspace exits with code 0
164	- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings exits with code 0
165	
166	**Checklist:**
167	- C10 — cargo check --workspace passes clean
168	- C11 — cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes clean
169	
170	## Boundaries
171	
172	- SHALL NOT add any third-party dependencies to beamr/Cargo.toml (dependencies like dashmap are added by the briefs that need them)
173	- SHALL NOT implement any logic in lib.rs beyond module declarations
174	- SHALL NOT add beamr-meridian to the workspace members (future brief scope)
175	- SHALL NOT implement loader, interpreter, or process logic — only the error type definitions they will use
176	
177	## Verification
178	
179	- cargo check --workspace passes with zero errors
180	- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings passes with zero warnings
181	- grep -r 'meridian\|yggdrasil\|norn\|libyggd' crates/beamr/Cargo.toml returns no matches
182	- crates/beamr/src/lib.rs contains only pub mod and pub use lines (no fn, struct, enum, trait, impl)
183	- beamr::error::LoadError, beamr::error::ExecError, and beamr::error::BeamrError all compile and implement Display + Error

output.content:
1	# ADR-001: Loader Is a Module Inside the Core Crate
2	
3	**Status:** Accepted
4	**Date:** 2026-05-29
5	
6	## Context
7	
8	The BEAM loader's outputs -- Module, Term, Atom -- are runtime types
9	defined in the core crate. We evaluated three options for where the
10	loader code should live:
11	
12	- **(A) Loader as a separate crate depending on core.**
13	  Illusory independence. The loader cannot compile without core, so the
14	  separation buys nothing except an extra crate boundary to maintain.
15	
16	- **(B) Shared types crate (beamr-types or similar).**
17	  Gravity-well problem. Term needs heap concepts, heap needs scheduler
18	  context -- the "shared" crate quickly pulls in most of core anyway.
19	
20	- **(C) Loader as a module inside core.**
21	  Simplest dependency graph. The loader is a consumer of core types and
22	  a producer of core structures. Colocating it acknowledges this reality.
23	
24	## Decision
25	
26	The loader is a module (`core::loader`) inside the core crate.
27	
28	No separate crate, no shared-types crate. The module boundary within
29	core is sufficient to keep the loader's concerns isolated. If a real
30	seam emerges later (e.g., the loader becomes independently testable
31	against a trait-abstracted runtime), extraction is straightforward.
32	
33	## Consequences
34	
35	**Positive:**
36	- Simplest possible dependency graph -- one crate, one compilation unit.
37	- No cross-crate type sharing or version coordination.
38	- Refactoring is cheaper: move a module, not a crate.
39	
40	**Negative:**
41	- Cannot compile the loader without compiling all of core. Acceptable
42	  because core compiles in seconds, not minutes, at this project scale.
43	- If the loader grows substantially, the core crate gets larger. This is
44	  a future-us problem with a known solution (extract to crate).

output.content:
1	# ADR-009: Reduction-Boundary Hook Is a Registration Point
2	
3	**Status:** Accepted
4	**Date:** 2026-05-29
5	
6	## Context
7	
8	At every reduction boundary (after each instruction or group of
9	instructions), the VM has an opportunity to perform bookkeeping:
10	check for pending signals, update counters, or emit diagnostics.
11	
12	The beamr-meridian crate needs to observe reduction boundaries for
13	telemetry and workflow diagnostics. However, core must not depend on
14	beamr-meridian or know what "diagnostics" means -- this is the project's
15	foundational dependency rule.
16	
17	Two approaches:
18	
19	- **Hard-coded call:** Core calls a specific diagnostics function at
20	  each reduction boundary. Violates the dependency rule.
21	
22	- **Registration point:** Core provides a hook seam. External code
23	  registers a callback. Core invokes whatever is registered (or nothing).
24	
25	## Decision
26	
27	The reduction-boundary hook is a registration point, not a hard-coded
28	call. Core provides the seam; what runs in it is registered from outside
29	by beamr-meridian or any other consumer.
30	
31	Core does not know what diagnostics are. It knows that something may
32	want to run at reduction boundaries and provides the mechanism.
33	
34	## Consequences
35	
36	**Positive:**
37	- Preserves the one rule: beamr-core depends on nothing of Meridian's.
38	- Hook overhead is zero when nothing is registered (a null check or
39	  Option::is_none branch, which the branch predictor will eliminate).
40	- Other consumers can register their own hooks for testing, profiling,
41	  or debugging without modifying core.
42	
43	**Negative:**
44	- Indirect call overhead when a hook is registered (function pointer or
45	  trait object dispatch). Negligible compared to instruction execution.
46	- Hook API must be designed carefully: it becomes a stability surface
47	  that external crates depend on.

## R5: LoadError type

### Files to create or modify

- Loader integration points:
  - `crates/beamr/src/loader/mod.rs:1-9`
  - `crates/beamr/src/loader/parser.rs:1-8`

### Current state
`crates/beamr/src/error.rs:1-7` currently only has comments and a scaffold function:

```rust
/// Crate-wide error types for beamr.
///
/// All runtime failures are represented as values, never panics.
/// Process-level errors become exit reasons; loader errors prevent
/// module registration; interpreter errors halt the faulting process.

pub(crate) fn _scaffold() {}
```

### Required type signature / shape
From brief:
- `pub enum LoadError`
- variants at minimum:
  - `InvalidFormat`
  - `MissingChunk(String)`
  - `DecodeError(String)`
  - `ValidationError(String)`
- implement:
  - `std::fmt::Display`
  - `std::error::Error`

### Patterns / conventions to match
- No dependency on `thiserror`; manual impls only, due R2 boundary.
- Loader responsibilities from design:
  - parser validates FOR1/BEAM and chunks: `DESIGN.md:101-106`, `DESIGN.md:337-341`
  - no panics/unwraps in production: `DESIGN.md:398-403`

### Implementation approach
- Replace `_scaffold()` with the real enum and impls.
- Use `use std::fmt;` or fully qualified names.
- Example shape:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum LoadError {
    InvalidFormat,
    MissingChunk(String),
    DecodeError(String),
    ValidationError(String),
}

impl fmt::Display for LoadError {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        match self {
            Self::InvalidFormat => write!(f, "invalid BEAM file format"),
            Self::MissingChunk(chunk) => write!(f, "missing required BEAM chunk: {chunk}"),
            Self::DecodeError(message) => write!(f, "failed to decode BEAM instruction: {message}"),
            Self::ValidationError(message) => write!(f, "BEAM validation failed: {message}"),
        }
    }
}

impl std::error::Error for LoadError {}
```

### Gotchas
- `MissingChunk(String)` is explicitly required; do not over-generalize it into an enum or `&'static str`.
- There is mention of unresolved imports in R5, but the minimum variant list does not include `UnresolvedImport`. Adding it may be okay, but not necessary for B-001; avoid creating payload types that later briefs must undo.
- Convert file-level comments in `error.rs` to `//!` or attach them directly to the enum; the current blank line after outer doc comments triggers clippy.

### Required type signature / shape

### Patterns / conventions to match
- Runtime failure names match BEAM/Gleam error atoms:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum ExecError {
    Badmatch,
    FunctionClause,
    Undef,
    Badarith,
    UserExit,
}
```

- Manual `Display` with stable human-readable messages.

### Gotchas

  - `Display`
  - `std::error::Error`

### Patterns / conventions to match
- Project principle is “No silent failures”:
  - `docs/design/beamr/DESIGN.md:74-76`
- Production paths should return errors instead of panicking:
  - `docs/design/beamr/DESIGN.md:398-403`
- Story S22 wants explicit error types:
  - `docs/design/beamr/USER-STORIES.md:51-52`

### Implementation approach
- Add after `LoadError` and `ExecError`:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum BeamrError {
    Load(LoadError),
    Exec(ExecError),
}

impl From<LoadError> for BeamrError {
    fn from(error: LoadError) -> Self {
        Self::Load(error)
    }
}

impl From<ExecError> for BeamrError {
    fn from(error: ExecError) -> Self {
        Self::Exec(error)
    }
}

impl fmt::Display for BeamrError {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        match self {
            Self::Load(error) => write!(f, "load error: {error}"),
            Self::Exec(error) => write!(f, "execution error: {error}"),
        }
    }
}

impl std::error::Error for BeamrError {
    fn source(&self) -> Option<&(dyn std::error::Error + 'static)> {
        match self {
            Self::Load(error) => Some(error),
            Self::Exec(error) => Some(error),
        }
    }
}
```

### Gotchas
- Implementing `source()` is not explicitly required, but it is the right error-chaining convention and costs nothing.
- `BeamrError::from(LoadError::InvalidFormat)` and `BeamrError::from(ExecError::Badarith)` should pattern-match to `Load(_)` / `Exec(_)`.
- Do not re-export from `lib.rs` unless desired; acceptance says `beamr::error::...`, not `beamr::LoadError`.
